title: Sour Gummies
excerpt: Sour Gummies. A home-cook's recipe from Rebecca's collection, kept in the family rotation.
sourceNotes: From Rebecca's personal cookbook, a recipe she's made many times in her own kitchen.

content.text: Sour Gummies. A British home-cook's dish, the kind of recipe families adjust across generations. Adjustable for what you need.
A weeknight-friendly recipe: most of the work is the prep, and the cooking takes care of itself.

attrs.body: 1 cup (200g/7oz) frozen fruit, defrosted (strawberries, blueberry, raspberry etc); 4 teaspoons unflavored gelatin*; 1-3 tsp citric acid.
attrs.title: Also

content.text: Puree the fruit in a food processor until smooth.
Combine all ingredients except gelatin, sugar (for coating) and citric acid in a small saucepan over medium-low heat. Stir with a whisk.

When it comes to a simmer, gradually sprinkle and whisk the gelatin into the mixture, stirring constantly. (Avoid dumping gelatin into pan all at once, which will cause big globs of gelatin to remain in finished product.)
Keep heating on medium-low until it just simmers and the gelatin has melted. The mix changes from grainy and jelly-like to glassy and smooth when the gelatin is fully melted.
Take it off the heat and pass it through a sieve to remove any gelatin of fruit bits. If there are bubbles on top, remove with a spoon.

Place gummy bear mold on a cookie sheet (which makes it easier to move without spilling once filled). Pour mixture into silicone mold and place in the fridge for a minimum of 6 hours. Any air bubbles that appear just pop with a toothpick.
Carefully pop gummy bears out of mold and store in an airtight container in the fridge. They will keep in the refrigerator for a few weeks.
Mix together the sugar and citric acid and then toss in the gummies until well coated. Store in a container on the counter top. Do not place in the fridge as they will get slimy.

attrs.intro: A few of the more common issues, and what tends to fix them.

items.fix: Salt a pinch at a time, tasting. A squeeze of lemon or splash of vinegar at the end lifts most savoury dishes.
Pull off the heat a minute or two before you think it's done. Plate straight away, or transfer to a warmed serving dish.
items.cause: Carryover cooking, food keeps cooking on the hot pan after the heat goes off.
items.symptom: It's overcooked by the time it reaches the table

items.fix: Drop the heat and give it longer. A probe thermometer is the only reliable check for meat.
items.cause: Heat too high, browned the outside before the middle warmed through.

items.fix: Reheat covered with a splash of stock or water, on a low heat. Microwave on 60% power if using one, gentler than full blast.
items.cause: Reheated uncovered, or no liquid in the pan.

attrs.heading: If something goes wrong

content.text: Vegetarian swap: replace meat with mushrooms, lentils, or beans, adjust seasoning to compensate.
Spice version: a pinch of chilli flakes or cracked black pepper at the end shifts the dish without losing its character.
Best fresh. Leftovers keep two days in the fridge; reheat covered with a splash of liquid.
A British home cook's dish. It sits among the recipes families remember without thinking about, the kind that gets adjusted across generations and never quite the same twice.
